items.text: Studies of real investors keep finding the same thing: the average person in a fund earns noticeably less than the fund itself. Same investment, worse result — because people pile in after it's risen and bail after it's fallen. It's not an information gap, it's a behaviour gap. The market quietly moves money from the jumpy to the patient, no skill required.

Here's the whole thing in one line: your return = the fund's return − whatever your buying and selling cost you. Every headline-driven switch, every 'I'll wait for things to calm down', every chase of last year's winner chips away at it. The uncomfortable truth: for most people, a calmer temperament adds more to returns than any amount of extra analysis.

Fees compound against you. A 1.5%-a-year charge on a 12% return doesn't cost you 1.5% — over 30 years it can quietly eat about a THIRD of your final pot, because it nibbles the growing base every single year. So check any fund's expense ratio (its annual fee). Low costs and patience beat cleverness far more often than anyone expects.
Average fund investors earn notably less than the funds they invest in. Primary cause?
options: Timing: investors buy after rallies, sell after falls
items.explanation: The behaviour gap: flows chase recent performance in and panic out at lows. The vehicle performs; the passenger keeps jumping off.

items.text: Which change most reliably improves a typical individual investor's long-run returns?
options: Fewer, calmer decisions — cutting the behaviour gap
items.explanation: Most underperformance is self-inflicted through activity. Temperament and inactivity compound better than added cleverness.

items.text: Roughly what does a 1.5%/yr fee do to a 30-year corpus compounding at 12% gross?
options: Costs about 1.5% of the final amount
Consumes roughly a third of the final corpus
items.explanation: Fees subtract from the compounding base annually: 12% vs 10.5% over 30 years ≈ 30× vs 20× money. The drag compounds just like returns do.

items.text: Look up the expense ratio (annual fee) of one mutual fund or index fund you own — it's on the fund page at valueresearchonline.com or the AMC's site. Is it above ~0.5%? Now picture that fee skimmed off every year for 20 years, compounding against you and costing a real slice of your final corpus. Write the number down. If it's a pricey active fund, ask: has it actually beaten a cheap index fund after fees? (~5 min)
